text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic adhesiolysis using a disposable shearer, which simultaneously coagulates and cuts tissue. The shearer's blades minimize lateral thermal spread as the surgeon carefully pushes the omentum down, avoiding the intestines below. The dissection is performed gently to maintain a bloodless plane.

The surgeon carefully separates the uterus from adhesions on the anterior abdominal wall, ensuring no injury to the bladder. A Foley's catheter is inserted into the bladder for protection. After dissecting the bladder adhesions, the uterus regains mobility, and the surgeon proceeds to dissect adhesions at the adnexa.

The surgeon carefully navigates dense omental adhesions at the level of the adnexa, taking precautions to avoid excessive coagulation that could harm the ovarian reserve. The adhesions are visibly causing tension on the peritoneum, which may contribute to the patient's abdominal pain.
The surgeon completes the laparoscopic adhesiolysis procedure, ensuring all adhesions are fully liberated, restoring normal anatomical mobility.
